text.title: WBS Decomposition Rules
text.summary: Four rules that make a WBS valid: noun-based nodes, 100% rule, 8/80 leaf sizing, and ID stability — plus the required overhead packages agents skip.

rule.statement: Every WBS node must be a noun phrase describing an output — never a verb phrase describing an activity.
rule.rationale: Deliverable names survive implementation changes; activity names do not. A WBS structured around activities must be rewritten every time the approach changes.

example.note: Activity-based — breaks when technology or approach changes.
example.code: 2.1 Design user authentication 2.2 Build user authentication 2.3 Test user authentication
2.1 User Authentication Module 2.1.1 Registration System 2.1.2 Login / Logout 2.1.3 Password Reset

rule.statement: Every parent node must represent exactly 100% of the scope in its children — no gaps, no overlaps. Verify by asking: "If all children are complete, is the parent complete?"
rule.rationale: Gaps produce forgotten work packages that surface during execution. Overlaps produce double-counted effort and budget disputes.

rule.statement: Always include these overhead branches; if any are missing, the WBS is incomplete: Project Management, QA, Deployment, Documentation, Training, Transition.
rule.rationale: Overhead is 15-25% of real project effort. Agents and manual drafters systematically omit it, creating a structural budget gap that cannot be recovered during execution.

rule.statement: Leaf nodes must have effort estimates between 8 and 80 hours. Leaves outside this range must be split (too large) or merged (too small).
rule.rationale: Below 8 hours, tracking overhead exceeds value. Above 80 hours, the estimate is a guess and internal dependencies are invisible. Calibrate bounds to match your tracking unit.

rule.statement: Stop decomposing when the 8/80 rule is satisfied. Maximum 4 levels for human readability; deeper decomposition belongs in the Dictionary, not the tree.
rule.rationale: Vanity depth gives a false sense of precision without improving schedule or cost predictability. Level 5+ trees are unmaintainable.

rule.statement: WBS IDs are append-only. Once assigned, a numeric ID never changes or is reused, even if the node is deleted or scope is reduced.
rule.rationale: Change requests, risk entries, and task files reference WBS IDs. Renumbering breaks traceability across the entire project record.
